text: Hello and welcome to This is My Architecture. My name is Derek and I'm here today with Chris
from New Relic. Welcome, Chris. Hey, Derek. Good to be here. Thanks for joining us today.
Certainly. So we're here to talk about the New Relic Synthetix testing product. Before we jump
into that, can you tell us what is synthetic testing and how is that different from unit
tests and integration tests? Sure. Great question. Synthetic testing, unlike real user testing
where your customers are hitting your site, is automated testing so that we can check
your website or your API on a schedule. Very cool. And that's simulating sort of customers
using the website from all different places around the world, right? That's right. So a
customer who's already built a test that they're checking out, say, in the desktop, can use the
same code. So we have a scheduler. They take, say, a JavaScript test for an API and they enter
it either through our UI or an API into our scheduler. That's where you take care of time.
Every minute, every hour. Once we know that, we can actually schedule, funny that, into a
job queue. The job queue is where those jobs will be stored until our runtime is able to actually
pick it up and start doing the tests. Once the tests are done, we get results back through our
monitor APIs and they go into a resulting queue where they can be analyzed, put on dashboards,
or alerted on. And then the whole process goes all the way back to the beginning and starts all over
again on the next hour or minute or whatever it is that you want. Great. So I can take my existing
test code, more or less the same, put it in the schedule, and it'll sort of take it from there. Correct.
So I see here you've got your monitors. They're actually doing the testing. Can you talk a little
bit about how those work? Sure. Well, there are two general cases you have as a developer. You might
be building a public website or API. So we'll take those jobs that you've scheduled. We run on your
behalf those run times and run that test for you. And that hits your public API or website. That's
really easy. And we run these things that are called monitors. Sometimes minions is our kind of fun name
for it. The problem is, what if you have a website that's private? It's behind a VPC or something like that.
In that case, we have our private monitors or minions. Those run inside of the customer's VPC or data center
or whatever they have. And they do those tests. It's all very secure. And then they have to pass their results back to us
in some way. Now it's tricky because of the security. Got it. And so just to make sure I understand the public monitors
are those are something New Relic's going to run on my behalf. But if I need to run something inside my private
network, inside a private VPC, that's where I would be running the monitors myself. Right. Now what we provide
are those run times in a container that our customers can run on whatever infrastructure they have. Very cool. So I see here you've got the AWS
Global Accelerator plugged in as well. How does that work with regards to the private monitors? Sure. Well, of course, the trick
is that these things are running inside of our customers' VPC or in a data center someplace. They need to have some sort of
secure, scalable, fixed way of getting back. We're assuming that they're using good IT and DevOps security practices.
Global Accelerator gives us a set of static IPs we can share with our customers. So these minions know how to
communicate to a fixed place. It's secure. It's a way for them to whitelist those IPs. It's performant across all the regions around the world.
That allows us then to route that traffic back to our monitoring API. And we can make whatever changes we want. Our customers
don't even have to know about it. They have those nice fixed static IPs that Global Accelerator provides for us.

Very cool. So you've got your sort of public facing endpoints and your private monitors as well. And then you can use both of these at the same time to monitor different aspects of your application. And then kind of have a single pane of glass to see how things are doing.

That's right. And what's kind of cool about this is those tests that maybe the developers were creating in the beginning, perhaps in their IDE or something like that, they can use the same exact tests, whether it's public or private. It doesn't matter. They'll still upload it either manually or through an API.
And they really don't have to be thinking about all the toil of setting up automated testing.

Very cool. Let me just ask one more question. So if I've got a continuous integration, continuous development pipeline going, can I hook this in so that every time I have a build it sort of doesn't update? Is that something customers do?
Oh, absolutely. I mean, who wants to do all this manual stuff either in a web browser or having to run a script every time or remembering to do that?
So what people will do at this point here where I have these double arrows is they might hook up their CI CD pipe.
And we have a RESTful API. So it doesn't matter what technology they use, whether it's an open source tool like Jenkins, some in-house tool, one of the Amazon services.
They can use our RESTful API. They can use Terraform. They can use any kind of scripting tool they're comfortable with to automate the reuse of their tasks into our runtime.
That's great. Well, Chris, thanks so much for joining us today and for sharing your architecture with us.
You're welcome.
And thanks for watching. This is my architecture.
